meta.note: FICTIONAL demonstration data. Not a real person, company or deal. Balance rule: no lane twice in one week; the calendar view flags violations.

items.title: Love Island USA and audience expansion
items.objective: Reach a broader audience without losing category credibility.
items.treatment: Use the finale as evidence that entertainment can pull prediction markets into a different audience. Avoid pretending one programme proves durable adoption.
items.verification: Confirm the post published and capture first 24-hour performance.

items.title: Polymarket Clearing rulebook changes
items.objective: Own a technical but commercially important live signal.
items.treatment: Frame the US build as a clearing, customer-protection and intermediated-access story. Use only if the changes are visible in official documents.
items.verification: Check the 13 July rulebook version and describe only confirmed changes.

items.title: Prediction markets creating jobs
items.objective: Use talent movement as a commercial signal.
items.treatment: Already scheduled. Treat hiring as evidence of category build mode, not as a generic jobs roundup.
items.verification: Refresh named roles if the draft cites live vacancies.

items.title: Love Island retention follow-up
items.objective: Turn a topical entertainment post into a retention question.
items.treatment: Ask whether the audience reached through entertainment trades anything else after the finale. Use a real platform, ranking or volume signal, not vibes.
items.verification: Confirm finale timing and any post-finale market or traffic data.

items.title: US CPI market check
items.objective: Show how macro event markets behave under a hard information release.
items.treatment: Capture the market price shortly before 8:30am ET, then the immediate repricing. Post only if liquidity and movement create a useful product lesson.
items.verification: Use BLS release time, capture timestamped prices and avoid claiming forecasting success from one print.

items.title: Weather markets and settlement sources
items.objective: Make contract design accessible through a topical example.
items.treatment: Already scheduled. Keep the focus on official settlement sources, sensor edge cases and why product education cannot live in a help centre.
items.verification: Check any named weather-market rule and source wording.

items.title: World Cup semi-final: same probabilities, different product
items.objective: Move the debate from odds to product experience.
items.treatment: Compare a prediction-market price with a sportsbook price at the same moment after removing bookmaker margin. Ask what differentiates the products if probabilities converge.
items.verification: Capture both prices at the same timestamp and document the margin calculation.

items.title: US PPI market reaction
items.objective: Test whether economic markets are becoming genuinely useful.
items.treatment: Use only if price formation, liquidity or settlement reveals something different from the CPI market. Skip generic macro commentary.
items.verification: Confirm BLS time and capture the market before and after release.

items.title: Prediction markets climbing the US App Store
items.objective: Show consumer discovery moving beyond the existing niche.
items.treatment: Already scheduled. Treat rankings as distribution evidence and avoid turning a temporary chart position into a durable adoption claim.
items.verification: Refresh rankings immediately before publication.

items.title: Prediction markets marketed like sportsbooks
items.objective: Open a sharper conversation about whether consumer perception is being set by affiliate mechanics rather than exchange architecture.
items.treatment: Use the New York Post giving Kalshi and Polymarket the same NYPMAX code, plus trade/deposit bonuses, to show how distribution can define the category faster than legal language.
items.verification: Capture both 13 July articles and quote the offers exactly. Do not generalise beyond the documented promotion.

items.title: Second World Cup semi-final
items.objective: Use live sport to explain product mechanics.
items.treatment: Choose one product observation: contract wording, exit behaviour, liquidity, settlement or market versus sportsbook pricing. No match recap.
items.verification: Verify live prices and market wording.

items.title: When do prophets profit?
items.objective: Translate rigorous research into a commercial market-structure question.
items.treatment: Use Makers and Takers to separate forecast quality from entry price, execution, liquidity, position sizing and hedging. Keep the hedging section careful and practical.
items.verification: Confirm paper title, authors, findings and any hedging examples.

items.title: CFTC deadline: ten-day warning
items.objective: Start shaping the regulatory conversation before deadline day.
items.treatment: Pull one substantive issue from the proposal, such as gaming definitions, public-interest tests or the process for reviewing contracts. Do not post a calendar reminder.
items.verification: Use the June 12 Federal Register proposal and quote narrowly.

items.title: Love Island and the spoiler supply chain
items.objective: Create a distinctive entertainment-integrity story that connects media, compliance and market design.
items.treatment: Frame reality television as an information-security problem: production staff, contestants, contractors, voters, fan communities, search platforms and AI summaries can all affect a market before settlement.
items.verification: Use the Vanity Fair reporting carefully. Separate allegations, public rumours and confirmed platform statements.

items.title: Forecasting, trading and hedging
items.objective: Gather practitioner evidence that the LinkedIn post cannot answer alone.
items.treatment: Ask what someone must understand before using a prediction market as a hedge: price, liquidity, basis risk, settlement, sizing and exit.
items.verification: Check the final Stuart post so the Forum prompt adds a new question.

items.title: The Odyssey release
items.objective: Build an entertainment lane without posting generic film promotion.
items.treatment: Use only if a box-office, awards or opening-week market has real liquidity. Compare the market signal with presales or reviews and ask what the contract can actually know.
items.verification: Confirm the live market, contract wording and official 17 July release.

items.title: World Cup third-place match
items.objective: Protect the feed from forced live commentary.
items.treatment: Leave open. Publish only if volume, pricing or product behaviour creates a point that has not already been made during the semi-finals.
items.verification: Check volume and whether the observation is genuinely new.

items.title: The retention test starts now
items.objective: Own the post-tournament category question.
items.treatment: Post shortly after the World Cup final. The tournament result matters less commercially than whether users trade another category next.
items.verification: Confirm the final has ended and avoid unverified volume claims.

items.title: World Cup numbers snapshot
items.objective: Create a factual baseline for the retention discussion.
items.treatment: Use confirmed final volume, app ranking, sports share or user data. Direction first, caveats where needed.
items.verification: Use original platform or reputable reporting and label reported figures.

items.title: Prediction prices inside ordinary sports journalism
items.objective: Gather newsroom, operator and data-provider views before building a formal citation standard.
items.treatment: Use Axios Cleveland treating Kalshi as one input in LeBron transfer coverage. Ask what standards should apply when journalists cite a live market price as evidence.
items.verification: Timestamp the quoted market price and note that transfer rumours remain unconfirmed.

items.title: The World Cup's real result comes after the final
items.objective: Turn a sporting moment into a serious distribution and retention post.
items.treatment: Develop the commercial distinction between durable users and tournament tourists. Define what evidence would change the view.
items.verification: Refresh post-final data and avoid over-reading first-day movement.

items.title: Goldman Sachs draws a contract-specific compliance line
items.objective: Move the compliance debate from generic prohibition towards workable policy design.
items.treatment: The useful signal is not a blanket ban. Goldman reportedly distinguishes sports and entertainment from company, election and financial-market contracts, suggesting risk depends on the employee-information-contract relationship.
items.verification: Label this as a policy first reported before 13 July and recirculated during the 13-14 July news cycle. Attribute to the Financial Times/Bloomberg reporting.

items.title: Post-tournament behaviour signal
items.objective: Keep the analysis live and evidence-led.
items.treatment: Use one specific signal: where volume moved, which categories gained or whether app rankings held. Do not repeat the LinkedIn post.
items.verification: Verify the signal and compare like-for-like periods.

items.title: What would prove the World Cup created lasting users?
items.objective: Generate useful operator benchmarks and debate.
items.treatment: Ask which metrics matter: retention, second-category trading, active days, deposits, cohort behaviour or acquisition cost.
items.verification: Keep the prompt short and avoid leading members to one answer.

items.title: North Carolina chooses taxation
items.objective: Explain how states can shape economics even under federal market structure.
items.treatment: Publish only after confirming legislation or budget language. Focus on what taxation says about category treatment and state-level commercial friction.
items.verification: Confirm signature, effective date and exact tax treatment.

items.title: When the market thinks, how many traders is that?
items.objective: Generate practitioner input for the Whale Consensus Illusion and participant-transparency work.
items.treatment: Use the UC Berkeley study highlighted by MarketWatch to challenge the assumption that a displayed probability always represents a broad, independent crowd rather than concentrated capital.
items.verification: Read the underlying paper before publication. Distinguish price influence, trade size and forecasting performance.

items.title: The CFTC argument now requires actual answers
items.objective: Position NEXTPredict around unresolved operating questions, not legal theatre.
items.treatment: Explain the practical issues the rulemaking must address: sports, public interest, contract design, manipulation, consumer protection and state conflict.
items.verification: Use the current NPRM text and do not overstate the Commission's final position.

items.title: NEXTPredict 60 pilot: what did the World Cup leave behind?

items.title: CFTC comment-letter observations
items.objective: Turn dense filings into timely intelligence.
items.treatment: Pull out one or two genuinely surprising submissions. Attribute every position and explain the commercial consequence.
items.verification: Read the original submission and separate commenter claims from Commission views.

items.title: CFTC prediction-markets comment deadline
items.objective: Own the biggest regulatory deadline in the near-term calendar.
items.treatment: Give one sharp read on what the submissions reveal about competing interests in the category.
items.verification: Confirm the docket is still open and use the official 27 July deadline.

items.title: 24/7 energy derivatives and perpetuals deadline

items.treatment: Use the parallel deadline to widen the conversation to reference-price integrity, surveillance, margin, clearing, settlement and operational readiness.

items.treatment: Focus on territorial rights valuation, access, advertising rules, contract protections and reputational exposure. Avoid anti-Polymarket framing.

items.title: The Probability Citation Tracker

items.verification: Refresh public examples of employer restrictions.

items.title: FOMC decision market reaction
items.objective: Keep macro commentary anchored to market mechanics.

items.objective: Show the category broadening beyond consumer brands.

items.title: Browser-extension distribution signal

items.objective: Own a distinctive distribution thesis.
items.treatment: Approved and banked. Publish only after a visible distribution trigger. If none appears, replace this slot with the intermediary-layer post.

items.title: AI crowds may still behave like one crowd
items.objective: Bring nuance to the AI-trader narrative.
items.treatment: Use research on correlated model behaviour to question whether automated participation creates real judgement diversity.
items.verification: Check the paper and avoid universal claims from a narrow study.

items.title: Who should own prediction-market discovery?
items.objective: Surface practitioner views on the intermediary layer.
items.treatment: Ask whether discovery belongs to the exchange, publisher, browser, aggregator or specialist research layer.

items.title: Polymarket may need to explain that Polymarket is different from Polymarket
items.objective: Turn brand structure into a category-formation question.
items.treatment: Explain US-versus-international brand architecture, product availability and customer confusion without taking a hostile stance.
items.verification: Verify current US operating structure and availability.

items.objective: Make a technical market-design issue understandable.
items.treatment: Focus on duration, settlement design, surveillance and reference-price integrity. Attribute the research and distinguish reported evidence from established fact.

items.title: Should ultra-short markets require different controls?
items.objective: Turn the X thread into a practitioner design discussion.
items.treatment: Ask about averaging windows, settlement sources, minimum durations and surveillance.
items.verification: Avoid duplicating the thread. Ask for operating experience.

items.title: US jobs report
items.objective: Keep the macro lane selective.
items.treatment: Publish only if the event market behaves unusually or reveals a wider liquidity or settlement issue.

items.title: The next prediction-market trader may not be human
items.objective: Connect AI to market structure and operating controls.
items.treatment: Explore automated execution, agent incentives and what changes when the participant is software. Avoid generic AI takeover language.
items.verification: Verify examples of live agents or clearly label experiments.

items.title: The exchange may not own discovery
items.objective: Develop the intermediary-layer thesis.
items.treatment: Show how analytics, alerts, publishers, browsers and aggregators could own the customer workflow even when exchanges own execution.
items.verification: Use current product examples and avoid unverified adoption claims.

items.title: US CPI market check
items.objective: Build a repeatable macro market-observation format.
items.treatment: Repeat the format only if it adds a new lesson versus July. Skip if it is merely another correct or incorrect forecast.
items.verification: Confirm BLS release time and capture timestamped prices.

items.title: LLM confidence is not trading skill
items.objective: Connect AI builders with traders and market makers.
items.treatment: Ask where model confidence ends and execution, sizing, liquidity and market impact begin.
items.verification: Verify the underlying research and state limitations.

items.title: The useful signal is the software appearing around the exchanges
items.objective: Position the summit around the category's picks and shovels.
items.treatment: Map data, surveillance, settlement, execution, compliance and market-making tools. Keep NEXTPredict as the natural room for this operating layer.
items.verification: Use credible current companies and confirm what each actually provides.

items.title: Post-World Cup retention check
items.objective: Create a repeatable data format with minimal production.
items.treatment: Use one verified cohort, ranking, volume or category-mix chart and ask what it really proves.
items.verification: Confirm methodology and compare equal periods.

items.title: NEXTPredict Settlement Ambiguity Index
items.objective: Create a defensible NEXTPredict benchmark for contract quality.
items.treatment: Score how difficult each contract is to understand, then test whether ambiguity correlates with wider spreads, complaints or slower settlement.
items.verification: Define a transparent scoring methodology and archive the exact rule version used.

items.title: The Price of One Percentage Point
items.objective: Create the most intuitive NEXTPredict liquidity metric.
items.treatment: Show how much capital sits behind a probability move and whether the move reflects broad conviction or thin liquidity.
items.verification: Use executable depth snapshots and state whether the move persisted.

items.title: The Reality Gap
items.objective: Make settlement mechanics understandable through real cases.
items.treatment: Show moments when the outcome people believed occurred differed from the outcome the contract was written to settle.
items.verification: Use only cases where the rule wording and official outcome are preserved.

items.title: Oracle Dependency Map
items.objective: Own the infrastructure layer behind settlement.
items.treatment: Reveal which agencies, data companies, publishers and committees ultimately define the answer across the category.
items.verification: Confirm every source relationship from live market documentation.

items.title: The Overtime Tax
items.objective: Build a recognisable sports contract-design franchise.
items.treatment: Compare similarly presented sports markets whose settlement definitions diverge once overtime, penalties or abandonment enter the picture.
items.verification: Align equivalent markets and preserve rule text before the event.

items.title: Contract Question DNA
items.objective: Bring behavioural and editorial analysis into market structure.
items.treatment: Test whether the way a question is written changes volume, error rates, spreads or disputes.
items.verification: Publish the classifier and manually validate a sample.

items.title: Weather Station Arbitrage Atlas
items.objective: Turn weather settlement into a visual, ownable data product.
items.treatment: Show how a city-level market can depend on one specific sensor with its own geography and data behaviour.
items.verification: Use official station IDs and document historical comparison methodology.

items.title: Settlement Source Reliability League
items.objective: Create an infrastructure benchmark that operators can act on.
items.treatment: Rank the external sources markets rely on by operational reliability rather than reputation.
items.verification: Separate source latency from platform settlement latency.

items.title: Payout Latency League Table
items.objective: Turn a hidden operational metric into a competitive benchmark.
items.treatment: Compare the operational journey from outcome to usable funds across venues and market types.
items.verification: Separate source delay, platform decision time and withdrawal time.

items.title: The Prediction-Market Change Log
items.objective: Build a uniquely defensible historical dataset.
items.treatment: Reveal wording and source changes made after trading begins, including clauses added during controversy.
items.verification: Store timestamped snapshots and distinguish clerical edits from substantive changes.

items.title: The Market Graveyard
items.objective: Build a historical archive competitors cannot reconstruct later.
items.treatment: Study product failures and governance decisions that disappear from the live market feed.
items.verification: Capture removal timestamps and distinguish voids from normal expiries.

items.title: Probability Half-Life
items.objective: Set a reporting standard for timestamping probability claims.
items.treatment: Show how long a market number remains useful after publication and what context was missing when it was quoted.
items.verification: Use the live market at the exact publication timestamp and document liquidity.

items.title: Probability Laundering
items.objective: Build a signature journalism franchise around probability provenance.
items.treatment: Follow a number from a small market move through headlines until it returns as supposed public consensus.
items.verification: Timestamp every stage and distinguish correlation from causation.

items.title: The Media-Market Feedback Loop
items.objective: Own the intersection of journalism and market reflexivity.
items.treatment: Trace how a probability becomes a headline, attracts new trading and returns to the media as supposed confirmation.
items.verification: Avoid causal claims unless timing and counterfactual evidence support them.

items.title: The Prediction-Market Journalism Audit
items.objective: Position NEXTPredict as a responsible category-builder with standards.
items.treatment: Assess how accurately major publishers use prediction-market probabilities in public reporting.
items.verification: Publish the scoring rubric and offer outlets a right of reply.

items.title: The Whale Consensus Illusion
items.objective: Challenge lazy media use of market consensus with evidence.
items.treatment: Separate broad agreement from a probability moved by one or two concentrated participants.
items.verification: Publish wallet-clustering assumptions and avoid naming individuals without proof.

items.title: Market-Maker Dependency Score
items.objective: Create a better quality measure than gross volume.
items.treatment: Identify markets whose apparent liquidity relies on one participant rather than a resilient two-sided ecosystem.
items.verification: Use behavioural signatures carefully and avoid naming firms without confirmation.

items.title: The Liquidity Family Tree
items.objective: Reveal concentration and identify the firms quietly powering the category.
items.treatment: Show where apparently competing consumer products rely on the same underlying institutions.
items.verification: Use confirmed commercial relationships and label inferred links clearly.

items.title: The Insider Proximity Score
items.objective: Turn insider-risk debate into a comparable framework.
items.treatment: Rank contracts by how many people may know the answer before the public and how easily that knowledge could be traded.
items.verification: Use process evidence, not accusations against named individuals.

items.title: Pre-Announcement Leakage Index
items.objective: Create a premium institutional intelligence product.
items.treatment: Compare prediction-market movement before material filings with stock, options and already-public information.
items.verification: Control for public rumours and use the official filing timestamp.

items.title: Prediction Markets in Corporate Filings
items.objective: Create an early indicator of institutional adoption and exposure.
items.treatment: Find where prediction markets first appear in official corporate language before they become headline strategy.
items.verification: Quote filings exactly and distinguish company statements from analyst questions.

items.objective: Bring institutional credibility and a clear market-structure question.
items.treatment: Find economically equivalent contracts and quantify where retail event markets diverge from conventional derivatives.

items.objective: Create a recurring, journalist-friendly market signal.

items.objective: Create a visual signature for each market category.
items.treatment: Show how different categories absorb information through jumps, gradual repricing or event-driven bursts.

items.objective: Create a measurable view of category identity formation.

items.title: The Talent Migration Graph

items.title: Kalshi flight-cancellation contracts hit a data-rights problem
items.objective: Open a serious conversation about data licensing, fallback sources and who controls the answer that settles a market.
items.treatment: Kalshi’s proposed airport-cancellation contracts look like practical risk transfer. The sharper market-structure signal is that FlightAware’s parent RTX reportedly says its data cannot be used for prediction-market settlement under its terms.
items.verification: Use the WSJ report and obtain the Kalshi contract rules or CFTC filing. Attribute RTX’s position precisely and do not imply FlightAware endorsed the contracts.

items.title: The CFTC just made contract finality the story
items.objective: Show why state-versus-federal conflict becomes much more serious once previously executed trades may be forced into liquidation.
items.treatment: The CFTC stayed Kalshi's emergency rule and directed it to fulfil open trades involving Michigan residents. Frame this as a market-integrity and contract-finality issue, rather than another generic sports-betting jurisdiction row.
items.verification: Use the official CFTC order. Distinguish the Michigan court order, Kalshi's proposed emergency rule and the Commission's stay.

items.title: Kalshi probabilities are appearing inside ChatGPT answers
items.objective: Own the immediate distribution signal and set up the developed answer-layer LinkedIn post.
items.treatment: OpenAI says some World Cup queries may show clearly labelled predictive information sourced from Kalshi. The user does not need to visit an exchange, understand event contracts or think of the number as a trading product.
items.verification: Use OpenAI’s official help article. State clearly that users cannot trade inside the answer and avoid unconfirmed partnership language.

items.title: Blockchain.com embeds Polymarket
items.objective: Ask who owns the customer relationship when discovery, funding and position management sit inside a distributor rather than the market venue.
items.treatment: The partnership lets eligible users access Polymarket through assets already held in Blockchain.com. The commercial read is distribution: prediction markets are becoming a feature inside existing financial and crypto products.
items.verification: Use the official partnership announcement. Confirm eligible-market wording and avoid implying universal availability.

items.title: Kalshi Pro and the institutional interface
items.objective: Lead the week with the strongest institutionalisation signal and create trader, infrastructure and sponsor conversations.
items.treatment: Kalshi Pro is the clearest current signal that prediction markets are building for professional workflow: multi-market monitoring, advanced order management and perpetual futures in one desktop environment. The commercial read is the interface, not the product launch headline.
items.verification: Use the company release and product screenshots. Distinguish public beta from a fully mature institutional terminal and avoid claiming institutional adoption without evidence.

items.objective: Show one category forming across product utility, data ownership, distribution and public perception.
items.treatment: Connect four current signals: flight-cancellation contracts as practical risk transfer, the FlightAware data-rights dispute, Kalshi probabilities appearing in ChatGPT, and the Buffett–Mansour framing battle. Use the CBS Sports state tracker as evidence that mainstream audiences now need permanent service journalism around access and legality.

items.objective: Own a non-obvious state-policy signal and show how revenue incentives can alter the regulatory conversation.
items.treatment: North Carolina has moved from debating classification to taxing prediction-market operators at 6% of net trading-fee revenue. Frame taxation as a commercial form of recognition, while avoiding the lazy claim that every state will inevitably follow.
items.verification: Use the signed budget reporting. State the 6% levy and 1 January 2027 start date precisely; do not describe the tax as a full licensing framework.

items.verification: Use Robinhood and CFTC sources for structure. Do not use the reported volume figures unless their methodology and routing basis are confirmed.

items.objective: Ask market makers, risk teams and researchers whether one risk model can sensibly cover sport, macro, politics and entertainment.
items.treatment: Use the new structural-volatility paper: volatility is highest near 50/50, rises near resolution and arrives differently by category. Sports look more jump-like; economics contracts look smoother.
items.verification: Read the full paper, reproduce only supported findings and clearly identify the Kalshi dataset and model limitations.
